name: Platform-neutral procurement language for AltID
consequences: Shifts procurement incentives toward open standards and fallback pathways, reducing platform-lock-in. Downstream, this can extend procurement cycles and elevate legal and contract-review costs as clauses align with standards instead of platform-specific signals. At the same time, it pressures suppliers to support multiple trust anchors, enhancing resilience but potentially complicating certification processes.
options: Mandate platform-neutral fallback authentication requirements in all AltID-related procurement documents and contracts.
Embed evaluation criteria that prioritize standards-based interoperability over vendor-specific trust anchors.
Prohibit exclusive reliance on native platform integrity signals by requiring alternative attestation sources and third-party certification for trust anchors.
review: Platform-neutral procurement vs. vendor lock-in risk. Weakness: The options may understate political resistance from incumbents and the urgency to preserve current trust signals.

name: Open Demonstrator Credibility and Standards Validation
consequences: Public phase-two demonstrators establish credibility for platform-neutral approaches and create tangible evidence of interoperability. Downstream, publishing results invites scrutiny and may reveal security assumptions, potentially prompting redesigns or scope reductions. Additionally, it increases pressure on procurement to translate demonstrator outcomes into policy language.
options: Publish phase-two demonstrator results with open-access briefs and invite independent reviewers to assess interoperability.
Release a hardware-token-based fallback blueprint and seek civil-society endorsement.
Establish an open conformance program aligned with EU standards to accelerate acceptance.
review: Demonstrator credibility vs transparency. Weakness: Public demonstrations may expose sensitive security assumptions or create scope creep if not tightly scoped.

name: EU interoperability and wallet standards engagement
consequences: Aligns Danish approach with European wallet requirements, increasing legitimacy and potential funding. Downstream, it may slow national policy moves due to EU timelines and risk of over-committing to standards before national procurement readiness. It can create pressure to adopt broader features beyond our fallback scope.
options: Engage with European working groups to encode platform-neutrality criteria in wallet standards.
Submit formal comments and contribute to NSIS/eIDAS alignment for fallback paths.
Coordinate cross-border pilots with other member states to validate resilience scenarios.
review: EU standards alignment vs national autonomy. Weakness: The EU process can be slow, potentially delaying concrete Danish procurement gains.

name: Resilience-centric policy framing and procurement leverage
consequences: Reframes the issue as continuity-of-service risk and supplier concentration, unlocking policy attention and potentially new funding. Downstream, it may trigger pushback from incumbents who claim cost and complexity, and could require robust evidence to sustain. It may shift attention from privacy concerns to resilience, requiring careful stakeholder management.
options: Frame the issue around continuity-of-service risk, supplier concentration, and procurement neutrality.
Develop a civil-society backed policy dossier to counter vendor-driven narratives.
Run scenario-planning workshops with public-sector decision-makers to stress-test procurement options.
review: Resilience framing vs cost/comfort of incumbents. Weakness: Narrative framing may not translate into binding procurement changes without concrete metrics.

name: Coalition-building and governance for platform-neutral agenda
consequences: Creates a broad coalition to sustain pressure and deliver decision gates. Downstream, governance complexity could slow progress and introduce conflicting priorities. It also builds legitimacy for procurement changes but requires ongoing coordination.
options: Mobilize civil-society, privacy advocates, and parliamentary digital policy committee to issue joint positions.
Create a formal governance charter with milestones, decision gates, and accountability measures.
Commission independent risk assessments and policy whitepapers to support negotiation positions.
review: Coalition governance vs political fragmentation. Weakness: Coalitions may fracture, delaying decisions and diluting messages.

name: Technical reference architecture and attestation framework
consequences: Outlines a concrete platform-neutral baseline for fallback authentication across devices. Downstream, it pressures vendors to adapt to a multi-anchor model and may require significant coordination with EU standards bodies. It creates a roadmap for testing, certification, and procurement language linking technical work to policy outcomes.
options: Define a reference architecture using NSIS and eIDAS-compliant open standards as baseline for fallback.
Develop a vendor-neutral attestation framework that can plug into multiple platforms.
Investigate privacy-preserving cross-platform credentialing and selective disclosure models.
review: Technical pathway for platform-neutral fallbacks vs integration risk. Weakness: Standards conformance may lag vendor support, causing compatibility gaps.

name: AltID platform-neutral fallback requirements in procurement language
consequences: Imposing formal platform-neutral fallback requirements in AltID procurement language shifts decision power away from platform gatekeepers toward broader trust-anchor candidates. Direct effect is to create a defined path for non-Apple/Google authentication channels to be considered in public procurement, reducing single-vendor exposure. Downstream, this fosters supplier competition and resilience, but adds upfront certification overhead and regulatory-readiness demands for potential fallback implementations.
options: Convene a cross-agency procurement workshop to define platform-neutral fallback criteria and map certification pathways across NSIS and eIDAS2.
Pilot a small-scale AltID procurement exercise that allocates budget for two alternative trust anchors and measures interoperability with fallback flows.
Commission a regulatory impact assessment to quantify transition costs and governance requirements of platform-neutral fallback adoption across critical public services.
review: Platform-neutral fallback requirements versus platform integrity signals. Weakness: The options overlook transition costs.

name: Independent standards-based fallback demonstrators for AltID
consequences: Launching non-production demonstrators creates credible evidence that standards-based fallback access can function without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google. Direct effects include clarifying technical feasibility and identifying integration gaps with OpenID Connect, WebAuthn, and alternative distribution channels. Trade-offs include diverting limited budget from policy outreach and potential misalignment with eventual production-grade requirements.
options: Establish two concurrent demonstrators—mobile-oriented and browser-based—to validate standards-based fallback paths against a mocked public-sector identity provider.
Engage independent security researchers to perform third-party audits on demonstrator security models and publish non-proprietary findings.
Create a regulatory briefing package that translates demonstrator outcomes into procurement-ready language for AltID strategies.
review: Non-production demonstrations versus live MitID integration. Weakness: Demonstrators may fail to capture real-world trust dynamics or operator incentives.

name: EU interoperability alignment through European Digital Identity Wallet engagement
consequences: Engaging EU interoperability processes aligns Danish goals with European requirements for portability, resilience, and platform neutrality. It increases legitimacy, potentially unlocks funding channels, and applies external pressure on incumbent platforms through shared standards. However, bureaucratic timelines may slow concrete national procurement decisions.
options: Submit formal inputs to European Digital Identity Wallet working groups to advocate platform-neutral authentication and certified fallback provisions.
Organize a Danish-EU roundtable to test cross-border fallback scenarios and gather feedback from member-state representatives.
Commission a comparative analysis of other member states' procurement language to extract best practices for platform neutrality.
review: EU-level interoperability vs. national control. Weakness: The options may not translate quickly into Danish procurement decisions.

name: Resilience-centric procurement criteria and continuity planning in public contracts
consequences: Integrating resilience criteria into procurement emphasizes continuity of access and reduces single-vendor risk. Direct effects include higher governance overhead and longer lead times, plus better alignment with risk management practices. Trade-offs include potential higher upfront costs and slower onboarding.
options: Rewrite public-contract templates to require platform-agnostic authentication pathways for critical services and define fallback SLAs.
Launch a resilience audit pilot across a subset of services to evaluate business continuity under platform disruption.
Establish a standing procurement steering group with cross-government representation to review platform-dependency risk quarterly.
review: Resilience criteria vs cost and speed. Weakness: It may slow procurement cycles and invite vendor pushback reliant on platform ecosystems.

name: Multi-vendor dependency reduction through diversified supplier engagements
consequences: Promoting multi-vendor engagement disperses risk and reduces dependence on a single platform monopolist. It can drive competition and innovation, but increases interoperability coordination and integration complexity across services. Long-term governance overhead may be required to maintain consistent standards.
options: Create a structured multi-vendor pilot program for identity-trust anchors, requiring compatibility tests with common standards.
Set up a regional sandbox for alternate app distribution channels, enabling controlled evaluation of non-Apple/Google paths.
Negotiate procurement terms that reserve capacity for fallback providers and require cross-vendor support agreements.
review: Vendor diversification vs consolidation. Weakness: Coordinating multiple providers may dilute accountability and reduce leverage.

name: Governance, transparency, and parliamentary coalition for platform autonomy
consequences: Formalizing governance and coalition-building creates political legitimacy and public accountability for platform independence efforts. It can accelerate policy adoption and ensure scrutiny, but may slow decision-making due to stakeholder consensus.
options: Establish a public-working group with civil-society, privacy advocates, and Parliament representatives to review procurement language and fallback requirements.
Publish quarterly transparency reports detailing dependency risk, vendor leverage, and progress toward platform-neutral objectives.
Coordinate a legislative briefing tour to secure parliamentary support for platform autonomy provisions in AltID strategy.
review: Governance vs speed. Weakness: The process may become a bottleneck if interests diverge.

name: Fallback authentication architecture using FIDO2/OpenID Connect across multiple device ecosystems
consequences: Adopting a cross-platform, standards-based fallback increases resilience by reducing single-provider dependency and expands access across multiple device ecosystems. A downstream implication is the need for governance and interop testing across platforms, plus potential certification overhead and timeline considerations.
options: Deploy a pilot fallback authentication architecture that relies on FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect across GrapheneOS, Android, and iOS environments to validate cross-platform viability.
Prototype a hardware-token-based fallback workflow that can authenticate without relying on any single mobile OS provider, reducing single points of failure while maintaining standards compatibility.
Institute a policy to document transition paths from current MitID-dependent flows to standards-based fallbacks, ensuring continuity during platform policy shifts.
review: Cross-platform resilience vs. OS-centric trust. Weakness: The options may understate regulatory, licensing, and certification hurdles that could delay implementation.

name: EU wallet interoperability framework alignment informs Danish procurement language
consequences: Aligning with the European Digital Identity Wallet framework reduces platform lock-in by embedding cross-border interoperability criteria into national procurement. Downstream, this creates political and regulatory leverage to push for neutral fallback pathways, but may require concessions on national sovereignty and extended negotiation timelines.
options: Lobby and collaborate with EU working groups to embed Danish preferences into the wallet framework, influencing interoperability requirements and procurement criteria.
Commission a comparative analysis of wallet governance models to identify where Denmark can secure platform-neutral fallback guarantees without delaying adoption.
Convene a formal, cross-ministerial working group to translate wallet interoperability expectations into binding procurement language and oversight indicators.
review: EU-alignment vs. national procurement autonomy. Weakness: The options do not fully account for potential shifts in EU standards or Danish regulatory processes.

name: Formal multi-stakeholder continuity obligations in procurement
consequences: Formalizing continuity obligations establishes explicit resilience expectations and triggers for transition if supplier concentration grows. Downstream, this can increase governance overhead and slow procurement cycles while improving accountability and risk visibility.
options: Establish a formal memorandum of understanding among civil society, academia, and government to codify continuity criteria and accountability timelines for identity access.
Draft procurement clauses that commit to minimum uptime and alternative-path availability, triggering staged assessments if supplier concentration risk materials emerge.
Organize quarterly coalition briefings to align policy proposals with evolving NSIS and eIDAS standards and gather feedback from stakeholders.
review: Continuity obligations across procurement cycles vs. bureaucratic inertia. Weakness: The options overlook transition costs and coordination overhead across agencies.

name: Open standard interface contracts for identity signals and attestation
consequences: Mandates open interfaces to enable plug-in of alternative trust anchors, enhancing verifiability and future-proofing. Downstream: increases initial negotiation complexity and requires robust documentation and privacy safeguards.
options: Draft contracts mandating open, well-documented interfaces for identity signals, attestation, and revocation to enable plug-in of alternative trust anchors.
Negotiate data-access and telemetry terms that preserve privacy while enabling independent verifiability of authentication events.
Pilot a standardized attestation framework compliant with NSIS to support platform-neutral trust anchors in pilot environments.
review: Open interfaces vs. negotiation complexity. Weakness: The options risk insufficient protection for privacy and data governance during interface standardization.

name: Multi-vendor demonstration program to test platform-agnostic access paths
consequences: Demonstration programs provide empirical insight into resilience across vendor configurations and user contexts. Downstream, they risk scope drift and require disciplined governance to prevent feature creep.
options: Launch a staged demonstration program across two public sector pilot sites to compare user experience and resilience under different vendor configurations.
Develop a risk map from demonstration outcomes highlighting policy, legal, and operational blockers to broader adoption.
Publish open demonstration results to inform procurement language and stimulate external scrutiny and feedback.
review: Demonstration-driven evidence vs. scope discipline. Weakness: The options may stretch resources without clear success criteria.

name: Independent risk governance including security reviews and privacy oversight
consequences: Creates external accountability and trust through third-party reviews. Downstream: potential delays and additional cost for audits, and need for clear authorization boundaries.
options: Install an independent panel of security and privacy experts to review demonstrator results, certify compliance with Danish and EU requirements, and report publicly.
Require periodic third-party audits of fallback components and their integration with existing MitID processes to preserve trust.
Create a red-teaming exercise program to stress-test continuity paths against regulatory, geopolitical, and operational shocks.
review: Independent risk governance vs. project timelines. Weakness: External reviews could become gatekeeping or create compliance fatigue if not properly scoped.
